Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Franz Brentano

1838 – 1917

Intentionality as the mark of the mental: the single idea from which modern phenomenology grows.

Brentano’s philosophy of mind is best read as a theory of conscious, object-directed mental acts. He calls the science of these acts “psychology,” but he means something closer to what later philosophers call descriptive phenomenology, philosophy of mind, philosophical psychology, and epistemology of consciousness. Its core claims are these: mental life is made of acts rather than faculties or hidden inner substances; every mental act is intentionally directed toward something; every mental act is also implicitly conscious of itself; the mind can be studied empirically through inner perception, memory, comparison, and indirect evidence from others; and the three basic kinds of mental act—presentation, judgment, and love/hate—ground, respectively, aesthetics, logic, and ethics.

The usual slogan, “intentionality is the mark of the mental,” is correct but too thin. Brentano’s deeper project is to build a science of mind that avoids speculative metaphysics, avoids reductive physiology, avoids Kantian transcendentalism, and still preserves the first-person evidence, normativity, and unity of conscious life.

1. Textual and historical orientation

Franz Brentano was born in 1838, trained in Aristotle, scholastic philosophy, theology, mathematics, and modern empiricism, and published the first volume of Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint in 1874. The later “volumes” and many works on descriptive psychology, time-consciousness, aesthetics, and logic are partly posthumous or drawn from lecture manuscripts, so any reconstruction of his system must distinguish Brentano’s published claims from later editorial compilations. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy warns that many Nachlass editions were prepared by students and later editors who sometimes adapted Brentano’s texts without marking all changes; it recommends treating works Brentano published himself as the safest textual basis.

Brentano’s immediate context matters. He is reacting against speculative “rational psychology,” against a purely physiological psychology associated with the rise of experimental psychology, and against Kantian restrictions on metaphysics and psychology. At the same time, he takes from Aristotle and the scholastics the vocabulary of act, object, and intentionality; from Comte and Mill the ideal of empirical science; and from the nineteenth-century psychology of association, psychophysics, and physiology the need to make psychology scientifically respectable. Treccani’s Italian entry usefully emphasizes this anti-Kantian, Aristotelian-scholastic, and anti-reductive setting, describing his psychology as a descriptive theory of psychical phenomena distinct from genetic psychology and from the natural science of physical phenomena.

A recurring interpretive problem is that Brentano became famous for a very short passage on intentionality, yet intentionality is not treated in Psychology with the systematic breadth later found in Husserl, Meinong, Twardowski, or analytic philosophy of mind. A German University of Graz note points out that the famous quotation is often treated as the “birth” of the modern concept of intentionality even though Brentano says relatively little about intentionality in Psychology; Dermot Moran similarly stresses that Brentano identified intentionality as the essential feature of mental phenomena but did not develop intentional description in Husserl’s later manner.

2. Psychology without speculative soul-metaphysics

Brentano’s first foundational move is methodological: psychology should not begin with the metaphysical question “What is the soul?” but with the experiential fact that there are mental phenomena. In the opening chapters of Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint, he says that even if one disputes whether there are souls as substantial bearers of mental states, one cannot coherently deny that there are mental phenomena; therefore psychology can be defined as the science of mental phenomena rather than the science of the soul.

This does not mean that Brentano was simply an eliminativist about the soul. He personally remained interested in immortality and the metaphysics of the soul, and later interpreters note that he did not abandon metaphysical concerns. The point is narrower: empirical psychology must not presuppose a theory of mental substance. It begins from acts and episodes that are given in experience. Contemporary scholarship on Brentano’s individuation of mental acts puts the point sharply: in the Psychology, inner perception gives us perceiving, thinking, judging, and similar acts, not a directly perceived ego, soul, or psychical subject; hence the early program is a “psychology without a soul.”

This move is one reason Brentano belongs simultaneously to philosophy and psychology. Psychology is empirical because it is based on experience, but the relevant experience is not primarily laboratory measurement. It is the disciplined analysis of conscious phenomena as they are given. Brentano also assigns psychology an architectonic role: logic, ethics, aesthetics, education, politics, and social theory all depend on understanding mental phenomena and their laws. In the primary text he explicitly says that aesthetics, logic, ethics, and politics have psychological roots.

3. The empirical standpoint: inner perception, memory, and indirect evidence

Brentano’s “empirical” standpoint is often misunderstood. He does not mean that psychology should be reduced to brain physiology or behavior. He means that psychology must begin from experience. The distinctive source of psychological experience is inner perception (innere Wahrnehmung), the immediate awareness one has of one’s own current mental life.

He distinguishes inner perception from inner observation. Inner perception is the immediate, non-inferential awareness built into current experience: when I hear a tone, I am not merely directed toward the tone; I am also aware of my hearing. Inner observation, by contrast, would be an act of turning attention toward a current mental act as an object of scrutiny. Brentano thinks this is unstable because the act changes when one tries to observe it. His standard example is anger: trying to observe one’s anger while it is occurring tends to alter or extinguish the anger. Thus empirical psychology relies on immediate inner perception, but systematic description often relies on memory of just-past experiences.

This distinction lets Brentano combine confidence and fallibilism. Inner perception, in the strict sense, has immediate evidence: if I am now consciously judging or desiring, I cannot be wrong that this mental act exists. But psychological science is not identical with momentary inner perception. It involves memory, comparison, conceptual analysis, and interpretation of other people’s speech and behavior; those are fallible. The Stanford entry notes that Brentano does not take ordinary descriptions of experience to be infallible, precisely because we cannot live an experience and simultaneously describe it with full observational attention.

Brentano’s method also includes indirect access to other minds. One cannot perceive another person’s mental acts inwardly, but one can infer them by analogy from expression, action, verbal report, and comparative cases. Denis Seron’s account of Brentano’s project emphasizes that psychological knowledge must include children, non-human animals, people with sensory differences, mental patients, and other subjects whose inner lives are accessible only indirectly through behavior and testimony.

4. Descriptive psychology and genetic psychology

Brentano later draws a central distinction between descriptive psychology, also called psychognosy, and genetic psychology. Descriptive psychology asks what mental phenomena are, what their elements are, how they combine, and what structural relations hold among them. Genetic psychology asks how mental phenomena arise, develop, and are causally conditioned, including by physiological processes. The former is prior because one must know what a mental phenomenon is before explaining how it is caused.

In German editions of Deskriptive Psychologie, Brentano defines psychology as the science of human mental life as grasped in inner perception, and says it seeks to determine the elements of human consciousness, their modes of combination, and the causal conditions of their occurrence. The same source distinguishes psychognosy and genetic psychology, treating psychognosy as relatively independent, prior, and exact.

The surprising part is that descriptive psychology is empirical and yet, for Brentano, can yield a priori laws. This is not Kant’s synthetic a priori. Brentano thinks that once concepts are clarified from inner experience, certain structural truths become evident: every judgment is either affirmative or negative; every feeling is founded on a presentation; some mental parts are separable while others are only distinctional. Seron stresses that Brentano saw no contradiction between empiricism and a psychological a priori, because the concepts analyzed by descriptive psychology are themselves drawn from experience.

So the Brentanian division of labor is this: descriptive psychology gives the taxonomy and structural grammar of consciousness; genetic psychology gives causal explanations of the occurrence, succession, and bodily conditions of conscious states. Brentano therefore does not reject physiology. He rejects the idea that physiology can replace a first-person descriptive science of the mental.

5. Mental phenomena and physical phenomena

Brentano’s distinction between mental and physical phenomena is not the same as the modern distinction between mind and external material objects. A “physical phenomenon” for him is often an object as it appears in external perception: a color, sound, spatial figure, warmth, motion, and so on. A “mental phenomenon” is the act of seeing, hearing, judging, remembering, hoping, fearing, loving, hating, desiring, or willing.

This means that a heard tone and the hearing of the tone are different kinds of phenomenon. The tone-as-heard is a physical phenomenon in Brentano’s technical sense; the hearing is a mental phenomenon. This usage can mislead modern readers, because “physical phenomenon” does not mean “mind-independent material thing.” It often means the phenomenal object of outer perception.

Brentano gives mental phenomena epistemic priority. In outer perception, physical phenomena do not reveal external reality as it is in itself; they are signs or appearances from which natural science infers causes. In inner perception, by contrast, the mental act exists as it is perceived. The primary text says that physical phenomena such as light, sound, heat, spatial location, and motion are not things that “really and truly exist” as presented, whereas inner phenomena are “true in themselves” and exist as they appear.

This gives Brentano a form of methodological dualism: mental and physical phenomena are radically different as objects of knowledge. But it does not by itself settle metaphysical dualism. He allows that mental and bodily states are correlated, that physical states can have mental consequences and mental states physical consequences, and that psychophysics and physiological psychology investigate boundary regions.

6. Intentionality: the mark of the mental

Brentano’s most famous thesis is that every mental phenomenon is intentional. In the canonical passage, he says every mental phenomenon is characterized by what medieval scholastics called the intentional or mental inexistence of an object, or, less ambiguously, reference to a content, direction toward an object, or immanent objectivity. Every mental act contains something as object, though not always in the same way: in presentation something is presented, in judgment something is accepted or rejected, in love loved, in hate hated, in desire desired.

“Intentionality” here does not mean intention in the ordinary sense of planning to act. It means aboutness or directedness. To imagine a golden mountain, fear illness, hear a tone, judge that no dragons exist, or desire justice is to be mentally directed toward something. Brentano’s claim is stronger than the modern claim that many mental states have intentional content. He claims that all and only mental phenomena display this feature.

The thesis has several layers.

First, intentionality is a criterion of the mental. It gives psychology its subject matter. Brentano’s aim in the famous passage is not primarily to solve every puzzle about nonexistent objects; it is to distinguish mental phenomena from physical phenomena and thereby secure psychology as a self-standing empirical science. Recent scholarship by Ion Tănăsescu defends this “minimal” interpretation: Brentano’s intentionality thesis chiefly separates the mental from the physical and grounds the distinction between mental and physical science, rather than offering a fully developed ontology of nonexistent objects.

Second, intentionality is internally differentiated. The same object can be presented, affirmed, denied, loved, hated, desired, feared, or willed. Mental acts differ not only by object but also by intentional mode. This becomes the basis of Brentano’s tripartite classification of mental phenomena.

Third, intentionality is ontologically ambiguous in Brentano’s early formulation. What is the intentional object? Is it an immanent mental object inside the act? Is it the external object as intended? Is it a content? Is it a non-existent object? Brentano’s students split over this. Husserl, Twardowski, and Meinong developed divergent theories of content, object, and intentional directedness. The IEP notes that some interpreters attribute to Brentano an early ontology of immanent intentional objects followed by a later critique of non-real objects, while others deny that there is such a radical change.

Fourth, the later Brentano moves toward reism, the view that only individual things exist. This makes him increasingly suspicious of entities such as states of affairs, propositions, universals, and merely intentional objects. The Stanford entry notes that this later reistic account is closely connected with his later metaphysics but does not straightforwardly solve the ontological status of intentional objects; it often reformulates rather than eliminates the difficulty.

The result is that Brentano gives modern philosophy of mind its central problem of intentionality but leaves multiple possible developments. One can read him as an immanent-object theorist, a content theorist, a proto-adverbialist, a relationalist about some acts, or, on the minimal reading, as a methodological demarcation theorist. The safest reconstruction is this: intentionality is the structural directedness of a conscious act toward an object-as-presented, without immediate commitment to the real existence of that object.

7. Consciousness and inner perception: primary and secondary objects

Brentano’s second major thesis is that every mental phenomenon is conscious. More precisely, every mental act is directed primarily toward its object and secondarily toward itself. When I hear a tone, the tone is the primary object of the act; the hearing itself is the secondary object. I do not need a second act of introspection to become aware of the hearing. The hearing is internally self-presenting.

The Stanford entry emphasizes that inner perception is not a separate higher-order act but a structural moment of the very act perceived. Brentano’s own example is that the presentation of a sound and the presentation of the presentation of the sound form one single mental phenomenon, conceptually distinguishable by relation to two objects but not two separate acts.

This is why Brentano is often treated as a precursor of self-representational or pre-reflective theories of consciousness. But his view is not a simple higher-order perception theory. Higher-order theories usually say a mental state becomes conscious because another mental state represents it. Brentano rejects that picture because it threatens regress and violates the unity of consciousness. Recent interpreters therefore describe him as offering a one-level or “one-and-a-half-level” account: there is a distinction between primary and secondary consciousness, but not a separate higher-order act.

Antonelli’s recent study formulates the point phenomenologically: each conscious act has its own “what-it-is-like,” and each form of intentionality has a phenomenal counterpart. Brentano’s inner consciousness is accessory, non-reflective, immediate, and intrinsic; it is not an explicit ego-awareness that precedes object-directed consciousness, but an accompanying awareness that occurs “out of the corner of the eye.”

This gives Brentano a distinctive solution to the relation between consciousness and intentionality. Consciousness is not a container in which intentional states occur. Consciousness is the self-aware occurrence of intentional acts. Every actual mental act is both world- or object-directed and implicitly self-aware.

8. Brentano’s rejection of unconscious mental acts

Brentano denies unconscious occurrent mental acts. He does not deny unconscious dispositions. One may have stored knowledge, habits, abilities, or character traits without currently thinking, judging, or feeling them. But for Brentano, an actual mental phenomenon is a conscious phenomenon.

The relevant question, as he frames it, is whether mental phenomena can exist without being objects of consciousness. He recognizes that the phrase “unconscious consciousness” is not a simple contradiction, but he ultimately rejects the postulation of unconscious mental acts. In the primary text, he distinguishes between actual unconscious acts and mere dispositions toward acts, such as acquired knowledge or character.

This is one of the least acceptable Brentanian claims for contemporary cognitive science and psychoanalysis. Modern theories routinely posit unconscious beliefs, desires, perceptual processes, affective biases, and subpersonal representational states. Kriegel notes that Brentano’s account of consciousness remains influential, but that his stronger claim that all mental states are conscious has few supporters in contemporary philosophy of mind.

Still, Brentano’s view has internal coherence. He is defining psychology as the science of mental phenomena as given in inner perception. If an alleged state is not even in principle inwardly perceivable as an occurrent act, Brentano will tend to treat it as a bodily process, a disposition, a causal condition, or a theoretical posit of genetic psychology—not as a mental phenomenon in the strict descriptive sense.

9. Unity of consciousness and the self

Brentano’s consciousness is unified but not simple. The mind at a time is not a heap of unrelated mental atoms. Simultaneous presentations, judgments, feelings, and volitions belong to one conscious whole. But that whole has parts. Some parts are separable, others are only distinctional; seeing and hearing may be separable components, while the awareness of seeing is not a separate act detachable from the seeing.

The German Deskriptive Psychologie explicitly frames the issue as “unity, not simplicity, of consciousness.” Moran likewise notes that Brentano emphasizes a real unity of present consciousness while allowing that consciousness is composed of parts, some mutually separable and others only one-sidedly separable.

This unity is not initially grounded in an ego-substance. Early Brentano does not find a self, soul, or subject in inner perception; he finds mental acts and their unity. Later, however, he moves toward a stronger notion of personal unity and, in his reistic phase, treats the psychological agent more like a true substance. Moran notes that in his 1901 outline of psychognosy Brentano speaks of each of us appearing to ourselves as a “personal unity,” and later comes to see the psychological agent as the only true substance.

This gives Brentano a complex position on the self. Against Descartes, the self is not the first object of inner awareness. Against Hume, consciousness is not merely a loose bundle. The self is approached through the unity of conscious acts, not through a separately perceived ego.

10. Time-consciousness

Brentano’s account of time-consciousness is crucial because intentionality is not limited to instantaneous objects. We hear melodies, perceive motion, remember past acts, anticipate future events, and experience temporal succession.

Brentano holds that strictly only the present is real, and that mental phenomena themselves do not have temporal extension in the way a melody does. This creates a problem: how can a momentary consciousness be directed toward a temporally extended object? His answer is original association or proteraesthesis: just-past phases of experience are retained in consciousness in modified form, so that the present act includes a temporal fringe of what has just elapsed.

He revised this theory repeatedly. At one stage, he treated temporal modification as a modification of the object; later he allowed that presentations themselves may differ in modes, including temporal modes. The Stanford entry stresses that Brentano never published a single stable systematic theory of time-consciousness, and that much of its influence came through students such as Stumpf, Marty, and especially Husserl, who used Brentano as a point of departure.

The philosophical significance is large. Brentano’s theory implies that consciousness is not a punctual flash. Even if only the present is real, experience is structured by retention, modification, and unity across immediate temporal phases. This anticipates later phenomenology of internal time-consciousness.

11. Brentano’s classification of mental phenomena

Brentano classifies mental phenomena into three fundamental kinds: presentations, judgments, and phenomena of love and hate. The division is not based on faculties such as intellect, feeling, and will in the older sense. It is based on distinct ways of being intentionally related to an object.

A presentation (Vorstellung) is the most basic mental act: the mere having of something before the mind. Seeing, imagining, remembering, and conceiving involve presentations. Every mental phenomenon is either a presentation or founded on one. One cannot judge, love, hate, desire, or will something unless it is in some way presented.

A judgment is not, for Brentano, the combination of subject and predicate, nor an attitude toward a proposition. It is an act of acceptance or rejection. To judge is to accept or deny the presented object. Brentano’s theory is “idiogenetic”: judgment is a primitive kind of act, not a compound made from presentations plus association. The SEP entry on his theory of judgment stresses that Brentano distinguishes judgment from presentation intrinsically and treats acceptance and rejection as basic mental attitudes.

This leads to one of Brentano’s most radical doctrines: existential judgment is basic. “A exists” means accepting A; “A does not exist” means rejecting A. Categorical judgments such as “All S are P” can be translated into existential form, for example: “An S that is not P does not exist.” He therefore tries to reconstruct traditional logic by reducing categorical propositions to existential judgments.

The third class, love and hate, includes emotions, feelings, desires, aversions, and acts of will. Brentano groups will with affective pro- and con-attitudes because all involve taking a positive or negative stance toward an object. Loving, hating, preferring, desiring, and willing are not reducible to judgments, but like judgments they can be correct or incorrect.

This tripartition is central to his entire system. Presentations ground aesthetics; judgments ground logic and truth; love/hate grounds ethics and value. The result is a philosophy of mind in which cognition, affect, volition, truth, goodness, and beauty are structurally connected.

12. Judgment, truth, and logic

Brentano’s theory of judgment is one of the most underappreciated parts of his philosophy of mind. Modern philosophers usually treat belief and judgment as propositional attitudes: to judge is to take a proposition to be true. Brentano rejects this as a distortion produced by language. In his view, the act of judgment is not fundamentally directed toward a proposition; it is a positive or negative stance toward a presented object.

This means that Brentano’s logic is psychologically grounded, but not in the crude sense that logical laws are mere empirical generalizations about how people think. Rather, logic studies the correctness of judgments and relations among judgments. He takes judgments, not propositions, to be primary bearers of truth. Validity and inference are grounded in evident relations among acts of judging.

His reconstruction of traditional logic follows from this. Universal affirmative judgments do not carry existential import. “All S are P” means that there is no S that is not P. Particular judgments do carry existential import because they accept something. This is why the SEP describes Brentano’s logic reform as an attempted overthrow and reconstruction of elementary logic.

There is also development and tension in Brentano’s view. Later he introduces a theory of “double judgment” for some predicative cases, where one first acknowledges a subject and then affirms or denies something of it. This complicates the early reduction of all judgments to simple existential acceptance or rejection.

The philosophical payoff is that Brentano gives a non-propositional theory of judgment. This remains relevant today in debates about whether perception, existential commitment, affirmation, denial, and predication must all be understood as attitudes toward propositions.

13. Emotion, will, value, and fitting attitudes

Brentano’s moral psychology is built on the analogy between judgment and emotion. Judgments are positive or negative—acceptance or rejection—and can be correct or incorrect. Emotions and volitions are also positive or negative—love or hate, attraction or aversion, willing or unwilling—and can be correct or incorrect.

For Brentano, goodness is not a natural property discovered independently of valuing, nor is it merely subjective preference. Something is good when it is worthy of correct love. The Stanford entry quotes Brentano’s principle: we call a thing true when the affirmation relating to it is correct; we call a thing good when the love relating to it is correct.

This makes Brentano a major precursor of fitting-attitude theories of value. A value is not simply what someone happens to like; it is what it would be correct, fitting, or appropriate to love. The IEP presents this as a way for Brentano to develop a psychological approach to ethics while breaking with ethical subjectivism.

The same framework extends to aesthetics. Beauty is linked to correct delight in a presentation. Brentano does not reduce beauty to arbitrary taste; he treats it as a form of goodness disclosed through a correct positive affective response to a presentation. The IEP notes that, for Brentano, we acquire the concept of beauty by abstracting it from experiences of correct delight in a presentation.

This part of Brentano’s theory is essential for extracting a comprehensive philosophy of mind. The mind is not merely a representational system. It is also a normative system: it affirms and denies, loves and hates, prefers and wills, correctly or incorrectly.

14. The resulting philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Brentanian philosophy of mind can be stated as a set of theses.

First, the proper starting point is not brain, behavior, language, or soul-substance, but mental phenomena as consciously experienced. Psychology is the science of these phenomena. It can remain neutral on metaphysical substance while still being realist about the occurrence of conscious acts.

Second, the basic units of mind are acts, not images, faculties, or hidden mechanisms. Seeing, judging, remembering, imagining, loving, fearing, and willing are acts or act-like phenomena. Their identity depends on their intentional object, their act-type, their mode, and their place within the unity of consciousness.

Third, all mental acts are intentional. To be mental is to be directed toward something as object. The object need not exist in external reality; hallucination, imagination, denial, fiction, and impossible objects remain intentional. But Brentano does not give a final settled ontology of such objects.

Fourth, all actual mental acts are conscious. Consciousness is not added to an act from outside. It is built into the act as inner perception or secondary consciousness. Every act is directed toward a primary object and implicitly toward itself as secondary object.

Fifth, consciousness is unified but articulated. A moment of consciousness is a real whole with parts. It is not a mere aggregate, but neither is it simple. Descriptive psychology studies the part-whole structures of mental life.

Sixth, mental life has three primitive intentional attitudes: presentation, judgment, and love/hate. Presentation gives an object; judgment accepts or rejects; love/hate takes a positive or negative affective-volitional stance. These three attitudes generate Brentano’s accounts of beauty, truth, and goodness.

Seventh, first-person evidence is indispensable but not sufficient for a full science. Inner perception gives immediate evidence of present acts; memory, comparison, conceptual analysis, and indirect observation of others support broader psychological knowledge; genetic psychology and physiology explain causal conditions.

Eighth, Brentano’s mind is not reducible to physiology or behavior. Bodily processes may condition mental acts, but the structures of intentionality, self-awareness, evidence, judgment, and correct love are not captured by third-person physiology alone.

Ninth, language is secondary to mental acts. Brentano’s theory of judgment treats many grammatical structures as misleading guides to the structure of thought. Propositions, predication, and existential import must be analyzed through acts of acceptance and rejection.

Tenth, the philosophy of mind is foundational for normativity. Truth, goodness, and beauty are not independent domains disconnected from psychology; they are grounded in correct forms of judging, loving, and presenting.

15. Strengths of Brentano’s philosophy of mind

Brentano’s first strength is that he makes consciousness philosophically central without reducing it to passive introspective data. Consciousness is active, intentional, self-aware, and structured.

His second strength is that he avoids two bad extremes: speculative soul-metaphysics and reductive physicalism. He brackets the soul as a starting point, but he also refuses to let physiology replace the descriptive study of conscious acts.

His third strength is the integration of intentionality and phenomenality. Many later philosophers separate “what a state is about” from “what it is like.” Brentano’s framework tends to bind them: each act-type has both directedness and a distinctive subjective character.

His fourth strength is his account of pre-reflective self-awareness. He sees that conscious acts are not normally objects of explicit introspection, yet they are not unconscious. This remains an important alternative to both higher-order theories and simple first-order representationalism.

His fifth strength is the act-taxonomy. Presentation, judgment, and love/hate provide a powerful architecture for analyzing cognition, perception, emotion, will, logic, ethics, and aesthetics in a unified way.

His sixth strength is the normativity of mind. Brentano sees that mental acts are not merely caused; they can be correct or incorrect, fitting or unfitting, evident or blind. This is especially important for judgment and emotion.

16. Major problems and pressure points

The first problem is the universality of intentionality. Are all mental states intentional? Pains, moods, anxiety, diffuse boredom, bodily feelings, and mystical states of “objectless” awareness are common counterexamples. Brentano can reply that even these involve an object broadly construed—a bodily region, one’s situation, the world as threatening, life as empty—but the reply is controversial. The IEP notes contemporary debate over apparently non-intentional phenomena such as sad moods or generalized jealousy.

The second problem is the rejection of unconscious mental acts. This is difficult to reconcile with psychoanalysis, cognitive science, implicit bias research, masked perception, and ordinary explanations of behavior by unconscious beliefs or desires. Brentano can preserve unconscious dispositions, but not unconscious occurrent mental states in the modern sense.

The third problem is inner perception’s alleged infallibility. Brentano is careful to distinguish immediate awareness from fallible memory and description, but the boundary is hard to maintain. People can misclassify emotions, confabulate motives, overlook background attitudes, and misdescribe their own experience.

The fourth problem is the ontology of intentional objects. If the intentional object is immanent, Brentano risks making thought too private: two people would not literally think of the same tree, but only of their own immanent objects. If the object is transcendent, hallucination and fiction become difficult. If the object is eliminated by reistic paraphrase, the apparent structure of intentionality becomes linguistically reconstructed rather than directly described.

The fifth problem is the theory of judgment. Brentano’s reduction of judgment to acceptance and rejection is elegant, especially for existential judgment, but strained for predication, relational judgment, modal judgment, mathematical judgment, and complex propositional attitudes.

The sixth problem is sociality. Brentano’s method gives other minds through analogy, expression, and report. That is useful, but it underdevelops intersubjectivity, shared meaning, social cognition, language, and the embodied interpersonal world—topics that later phenomenologists and analytic philosophers expand.

The seventh problem is the treatment of outer perception. Brentano’s claim that physical phenomena are not true in themselves gives psychology epistemic privilege, but it can seem too dismissive of perceptual contact with the world. Later direct realists and phenomenologists would resist his sharp asymmetry between inner and outer perception.

17. What non-English reception adds

German-language discussions often correct an English-language tendency to identify Brentano almost exclusively with intentionality. The Graz note emphasizes that the famous intentionality passage became emblematic even though Brentano did not elaborate intentionality at great length in Psychology. German editions and previews of Deskriptive Psychologie also foreground psychognosy, genetic psychology, exactness, part-whole analysis, and the methodological machinery of descriptive psychology.

Spanish reception is especially important through Ortega y Gasset and the Revista de Occidente. A Spanish article on Brentano and Ortega notes that Brentano’s Psychology appeared in Spanish in 1926, translated by José Gaos, reviewed by Xavier Zubiri, and framed by Ortega’s project of disseminating German and phenomenological thought in Spain. The article argues that Ortega’s presentation of Brentano was not merely ceremonial but supplied a key to understanding Ortega’s own thought.

Portuguese and Brazilian discussions often stress Brentano’s distinction between inner and outer perception and its consequences for psychology. A Brazilian article on Brentano’s distinction between physical and psychical phenomena says that psychology’s phenomena are supplied by inner perception, that inner perception has immediate evidence, and that outer perception is not “true perception” in the strict Brentanian sense because physical phenomena do not exist outside us in the same way mental phenomena do. Another Portuguese-language article frames intentionality as a description of consciousness rather than as a property of a special kind of object.

Italian reference material, especially Treccani, is useful because it situates Brentano against Kantianism and in continuity with Aristotle and scholasticism, while also noting the later mature doctrine in which the intentional object is interpreted in connection with what is real or thing-like. This helps prevent a purely Anglophone reading in which Brentano is treated only as a precursor of analytic intentionality debates.

18. Final synthesis

Brentano’s philosophy of mind can be condensed into one formula: the mind is the unified field of conscious intentional acts, given with immediate self-awareness and structured by primitive modes of presentation, acceptance/rejection, and love/hate.

From that formula, nearly all of his system follows. Psychology is the descriptive and genetic science of those acts. Intentionality distinguishes them from physical phenomena. Inner perception gives them first-person evidence. Secondary consciousness explains self-awareness without regress. The tripartition of acts grounds logic, ethics, and aesthetics. Time-consciousness explains how acts retain and modify what has just passed. Unity of consciousness explains why mental life is not a heap. The bracketing of the soul allows psychology to be empirical without being materialist.

Brentano’s system remains powerful because it treats mind as simultaneously intentional, phenomenal, epistemic, affective, volitional, normative, and unified. Its weaknesses are equally clear: it overstates the transparency of consciousness, rejects unconscious mentality too strongly, leaves intentional objects ontologically unstable, and relies on a first-person method that later psychology found difficult to operationalize.

For a contemporary philosophy of mind, the most fruitful Brentanian inheritance is not the exact letter of every doctrine. It is the project: a rigorous descriptive science of conscious acts that takes first-person evidence seriously while connecting it to logic, value, embodiment, and causal psychology.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
